purpose: Identifies correlation regime transitions using term premium levels as the cleanest leading indicator, answering: are we in a negative, transitional, or positive correlation regime?
components: Term premium as direct measure of compensation for duration risk — the mechanism through which fiscal/supply dynamics transmit to correlation
Historical thresholds: ~0bp or below → negative correlation regime; 50-100bp → transitional/unstable; ~150bp+ → established positive correlation regime
ACM term premium model as primary measurement tool
12-18 month lead time observed in historical regime transitions (1967-68, 1998-2000)
current_assessment: ACM term premium at 50-80bp (early 2026) is in the transitional zone — consistent with mid-transition but below the ~150bp+ historically associated with a fully-established positive regime. The trajectory (rising from -50bp in 2020) suggests upward momentum, but has not yet crossed the threshold for confirmed regime change.
limitations: Threshold levels (~150bp) are approximate and drawn from a different market structure
Risk parity, basis trade, and leveraged structures may amplify term premium moves, lowering the effective threshold
Only three historical calibration points with fundamentally different macro contexts
ACM model itself has parameter uncertainty and revision risk
